acm-certificate-with-transparency-loggin.name: Acm Certificate With Transparency Logging Disabled
acm-certificate-with-transparency-loggin.description: The ACM certificate has Certificate Transparency logging disabled. The TLS configuration on the flagged endpoint accepts protocol versions or cipher suites AWS has deprecated, or relies on a certificate the browser/AWS trust store no longer accepts. AWS publishes managed security policies that track current guidance — using one is the supported path.
acm-certificate-with-transparency-loggin.risk: Old TLS versions (1.0/1.1) and weak ciphers are vulnerable to downgrade and padding-oracle attacks that retrieve plaintext from intercepted traffic. Deprecated certificates fail validation as clients update, eventually causing a production outage on top of the security gap.

cloudformation-stack-with-role.name: Cloudformation Stack With Role
cloudformation-stack-with-role.description: The CloudFormation stack runs with an assumed IAM role. The flagged resource isn't an immediate security exposure but it carries operational debt — unused capacity, unowned configuration, mismatched naming. AWS guidance is to keep the inventory tight so reviews remain trustworthy.
cloudformation-stack-with-role.risk: Operational debt accumulates until reviewers stop trusting the inventory and real findings get lost in the noise. The fix is incremental and low-risk; the long-run payoff is a cleaner attack surface and faster reviews.

cloudfront-distribution-cleartext-origin.name: Cloudfront Distribution Cleartext Origin
cloudfront-distribution-cleartext-origin.description: The CloudFront distribution fetches from its origin over cleartext HTTP. The flagged endpoint accepts unencrypted HTTP / cleartext traffic — either as the only protocol, or alongside HTTPS without a redirect. AWS guidance is to terminate TLS at the edge and force every connection through it.
cloudfront-distribution-cleartext-origin.risk: Cleartext traffic means anyone on the network path — a misconfigured wifi captive portal, a hostile cafe, a downstream router — can read or modify the payload. Even endpoints that only carry public data leak credentials in cookies, auth headers, and query strings.

cloudfront-distribution-insecure-origin.name: Cloudfront Distribution Insecure Origin
cloudfront-distribution-insecure-origin.description: The CloudFront distribution accepts insecure origin protocols. The flagged endpoint accepts unencrypted HTTP / cleartext traffic — either as the only protocol, or alongside HTTPS without a redirect. AWS guidance is to terminate TLS at the edge and force every connection through it.
cloudfront-distribution-insecure-origin.risk: Cleartext traffic means anyone on the network path — a misconfigured wifi captive portal, a hostile cafe, a downstream router — can read or modify the payload. Even endpoints that only carry public data leak credentials in cookies, auth headers, and query strings.

cloudfront-distribution-insufficient-vie.name: Cloudfront Distribution Insufficient Viewer Security
cloudfront-distribution-insufficient-vie.description: The CloudFront viewer security policy allows weak TLS configurations. The TLS configuration on the flagged endpoint accepts protocol versions or cipher suites AWS has deprecated, or relies on a certificate the browser/AWS trust store no longer accepts. AWS publishes managed security policies that track current guidance — using one is the supported path.
cloudfront-distribution-insufficient-vie.risk: Old TLS versions (1.0/1.1) and weak ciphers are vulnerable to downgrade and padding-oracle attacks that retrieve plaintext from intercepted traffic. Deprecated certificates fail validation as clients update, eventually causing a production outage on top of the security gap.

cloudtrail-duplicated-global-services-lo.name: Cloudtrail Duplicated Global Services Logging
cloudtrail-duplicated-global-services-lo.description: Global-services CloudTrail events are logged by multiple trails (duplicate billing + log noise). AWS audit and access-log streams (CloudTrail, S3 access logs, ELB access logs, VPC flow logs, Config) are off or missing on the flagged surface. AWS guidance is to centralize these into a dedicated security-account S3 bucket with KMS encryption and integrity validation.
cloudtrail-duplicated-global-services-lo.risk: Without complete logs, every post-incident investigation hits a wall — there is no record of who did what, when, or from where. Compliance frameworks treat missing audit trail as a high-severity finding because it forecloses detection AND response.

cloudtrail-no-cloudwatch-integration.name: Cloudtrail No Cloudwatch Integration
cloudtrail-no-cloudwatch-integration.description: CloudTrail is not delivering events to CloudWatch Logs. CloudTrail's configuration is the trail audit infrastructure depends on — log-file integrity validation, CloudWatch integration, KMS encryption, and global-services capture all need to be enabled deliberately for the trail to be credible during an investigation.
cloudtrail-no-cloudwatch-integration.risk: A misconfigured CloudTrail is worse than no trail at all — defenders rely on it during incident response, and partial-data / no-integrity / no-encryption gaps create blind spots an attacker can exploit (and, with KMS-decrypt access, potentially tamper with after the fact).
cloudtrail-no-cloudwatch-integration.remediation: Configure each Trail to have a CloudWatch Logs group attached

cloudtrail-no-data-logging.name: Cloudtrail No Data Logging
cloudtrail-no-data-logging.description: CloudTrail is not capturing data-event activity (S3 / Lambda / DynamoDB). AWS audit and access-log streams (CloudTrail, S3 access logs, ELB access logs, VPC flow logs, Config) are off or missing on the flagged surface. AWS guidance is to centralize these into a dedicated security-account S3 bucket with KMS encryption and integrity validation.
cloudtrail-no-data-logging.risk: Without complete logs, every post-incident investigation hits a wall — there is no record of who did what, when, or from where. Compliance frameworks treat missing audit trail as a high-severity finding because it forecloses detection AND response.

cloudtrail-no-encryption-with-kms.name: Cloudtrail No Encryption With Kms
cloudtrail-no-encryption-with-kms.description: CloudTrail log files are not KMS-encrypted at rest. AWS provides encryption-at-rest as a first-class default for every storage service involved here (S3, EBS, RDS, Redshift, DynamoDB, SQS, …) via either SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, or a customer-managed CMK. The flagged resource is storing data unencrypted at the storage layer.
cloudtrail-no-encryption-with-kms.risk: Unencrypted storage means a snapshot leak, a disk-level forensic recovery, or a misrouted backup yields data in plaintext. KMS-based encryption also lets the key policy revoke access en-masse during incident response — without it, key rotation is meaningless.
cloudtrail-no-encryption-with-kms.remediation: Ensure each Trail is encrypted with a KMS key

cloudtrail-no-global-services-logging.name: Cloudtrail No Global Services Logging
cloudtrail-no-global-services-logging.description: CloudTrail is not capturing global-service events (IAM, CloudFront, Route 53). AWS audit and access-log streams (CloudTrail, S3 access logs, ELB access logs, VPC flow logs, Config) are off or missing on the flagged surface. AWS guidance is to centralize these into a dedicated security-account S3 bucket with KMS encryption and integrity validation.
cloudtrail-no-global-services-logging.risk: Without complete logs, every post-incident investigation hits a wall — there is no record of who did what, when, or from where. Compliance frameworks treat missing audit trail as a high-severity finding because it forecloses detection AND response.

cloudtrail-no-log-file-validation.name: Cloudtrail No Log File Validation
cloudtrail-no-log-file-validation.description: CloudTrail log-file integrity validation is disabled. cloudtrail-no-log-file-validation.risk: A misconfigured CloudTrail is worse than no trail at all — defenders rely on it during incident response, and partial-data / no-integrity / no-encryption gaps create blind spots an attacker can exploit (and, with KMS-decrypt access, potentially tamper with after the fact).
cloudtrail-no-log-file-validation.remediation: Ensure that each Trail has Enable log file validation set to Yes

cloudtrail-no-logging.name: Cloudtrail No Logging
cloudtrail-no-logging.description: The CloudTrail trail is not actively logging. AWS audit and access-log streams (CloudTrail, S3 access logs, ELB access logs, VPC flow logs, Config) are off or missing on the flagged surface. AWS guidance is to centralize these into a dedicated security-account S3 bucket with KMS encryption and integrity validation.
cloudtrail-no-logging.risk: Without complete logs, every post-incident investigation hits a wall — there is no record of who did what, when, or from where. Compliance frameworks treat missing audit trail as a high-severity finding because it forecloses detection AND response.
cloudtrail-no-logging.remediation: Configure all Trails to enable Logging, set Apply trail to all regions and ensure that Read/Write Events are set to ALL

cloudwatch-alarm-without-actions.name: Cloudwatch Alarm Without Actions
cloudwatch-alarm-without-actions.description: A CloudWatch alarm has no alarm actions configured. CIS-recommended CloudWatch alarms cover the security-relevant API surface — IAM changes, sign-in failures, root usage, NACL/SG/route-table edits, CMK deletion. Without alarms wired to a real notification channel the audit log is data nobody reads.
cloudwatch-alarm-without-actions.risk: Detection coverage is the gap between an attacker's first move and the responder's first action. Missing alarms on the API calls that signal compromise (CreateUser, DeleteTrail, root-account sign-in, unauthorized API calls) extend the window where an intrusion goes unanswered.

config-recorder-not-configured.name: Config Recorder Not Configured
config-recorder-not-configured.description: AWS Config recorder is not running in this account / region. AWS audit and access-log streams (CloudTrail, S3 access logs, ELB access logs, VPC flow logs, Config) are off or missing on the flagged surface. AWS guidance is to centralize these into a dedicated security-account S3 bucket with KMS encryption and integrity validation.
config-recorder-not-configured.risk: Without complete logs, every post-incident investigation hits a wall — there is no record of who did what, when, or from where. Compliance frameworks treat missing audit trail as a high-severity finding because it forecloses detection AND response.
config-recorder-not-configured.remediation: Enable AWS Config in all regions, define the resources you want to record in each region and include global resources (IAM resources)

ec2-ami-public.name: Ec2 Ami Public
ec2-ami-public.description: An EC2 AMI is shared publicly. The flagged resource is reachable from outside the AWS account or exposes data to anonymous principals (`Principal: "*"`, a public ACL, a publicly routable IP, or a public snapshot). AWS guidance is to confine the resource to a private subnet, a specific account, or a named principal list.
ec2-ami-public.risk: Publicly exposed AWS resources are continuously scanned by attackers — Shodan, censys, and dedicated bucket-enumeration tools find them within minutes of exposure. The most common AWS data breaches start as misconfigured public buckets or snapshots.

ec2-default-security-group-in-use.name: Ec2 Default Security Group In Use
ec2-default-security-group-in-use.description: The default VPC security group is attached to a workload. The security group rule is wider than the workload behind it needs — a `0.0.0.0/0` ingress on an administrative port, a default SG carrying real rules, or a long-unused SG kept around as dead config. AWS guidance is to scope every rule to specific CIDRs, principals, or SG references.
ec2-default-security-group-in-use.risk: Wide-open security group rules give attackers direct network reach to backend services that were never hardened for the public internet — SSH brute force, RDP attacks, exposed database ports, unauthenticated metadata endpoints. The exposure persists 24/7.
ec2-default-security-group-in-use.remediation: Ensure resources are not within default security groups. Instead, create a custom security group tailored to each resource needs.

ec2-default-security-group-with-rules.name: Ec2 Default Security Group With Rules
ec2-default-security-group-with-rules.description: The default VPC security group carries custom rules. The security group rule is wider than the workload behind it needs — a `0.0.0.0/0` ingress on an administrative port, a default SG carrying real rules, or a long-unused SG kept around as dead config. AWS guidance is to scope every rule to specific CIDRs, principals, or SG references.
ec2-default-security-group-with-rules.risk: Wide-open security group rules give attackers direct network reach to backend services that were never hardened for the public internet — SSH brute force, RDP attacks, exposed database ports, unauthenticated metadata endpoints. The exposure persists 24/7.
ec2-default-security-group-with-rules.remediation: Ensure the default security group of every VPC restricts all traffic

ec2-ebs-default-encryption-disabled.name: Ec2 Ebs Default Encryption Disabled
ec2-ebs-default-encryption-disabled.description: EBS default encryption is disabled in this region. AWS provides encryption-at-rest as a first-class default for every storage service involved here (S3, EBS, RDS, Redshift, DynamoDB, SQS, …) via either SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, or a customer-managed CMK. ec2-ebs-default-encryption-disabled.remediation: Enable encryption by default for EBS volumes in all regions.

The flagged surface puts data in places it shouldn't appear — credentials in EC2 user-data, secrets in container env-vars, MFA-Delete off on an S3 bucket holding durable state. AWS guidance is to keep secrets in Secrets Manager / Parameter Store and to lock buckets with MFA-Delete + versioning.
ec2-instance-with-user-data-secrets.risk: Misplaced secrets are extracted by routine internal access — any read on the EC2 metadata service or the bucket reveals the secret. Versioning + MFA-Delete protects against the worst case where an attacker tries to cover their tracks by overwriting evidence.

elbv2-no-deletion-protection.name: Elbv2 No Deletion Protection
elbv2-no-deletion-protection.description: The ALB / NLB has deletion protection disabled. AWS-managed data stores ship automatic backup and recovery primitives (snapshots, PITR, cross-AZ replicas, versioning, deletion protection) but they have to be enabled per-resource. Default-off behaviors leave the resource one operator-error or one ransomware event away from data loss.
elbv2-no-deletion-protection.risk: A resource without backups, retention, or deletion protection is a single incident from permanent loss — and the incident isn't usually malicious. Accidental DROP TABLE, accidental terraform destroy, accidental S3 object overwrite all produce the same blast radius.

iam-assume-role-lacks-external-id-and-mf.name: Iam Assume Role Lacks External Id And Mfa
iam-assume-role-lacks-external-id-and-mf.description: An IAM role's trust policy allows cross-account assumption without an ExternalId or MFA. AWS does not require multi-factor authentication on a console- or API-facing principal by default — it has to be enforced via IAM policy, Identity Center, or an organization-wide SCP. The flagged principal is currently protected by a single factor.
iam-assume-role-lacks-external-id-and-mf.risk: Single-factor authentication on an AWS principal is one phishing email or one leaked-password-database hit away from a full takeover. AWS incident response data consistently shows missing MFA as the top initial-access vector for cloud account compromise.

iam-assume-role-no-mfa.name: Iam Assume Role No Mfa
iam-assume-role-no-mfa.description: An IAM role's trust policy allows assumption without an MFA condition. AWS does not require multi-factor authentication on a console- or API-facing principal by default — it has to be enforced via IAM policy, Identity Center, or an organization-wide SCP. The flagged principal is currently protected by a single factor.
iam-assume-role-no-mfa.risk: Single-factor authentication on an AWS principal is one phishing email or one leaked-password-database hit away from a full takeover. AWS incident response data consistently shows missing MFA as the top initial-access vector for cloud account compromise.

iam-assume-role-policy-allows-all.name: Iam Assume Role Policy Allows All
iam-assume-role-policy-allows-all.description: An IAM role's trust policy allows assumption from any principal. The flagged policy grants more authority than the workload appears to need (full-action wildcards, NotAction-style allow lists, or unbounded resource selectors). The principle of least privilege requires the policy be narrowed to the specific actions and resources the workload actually uses.
iam-assume-role-policy-allows-all.risk: Over-broad IAM permissions turn any single compromised credential into a foothold for lateral movement across the account. Attackers routinely enumerate attached policies and pivot from one over-permissioned role to another to reach data, secrets, or billing.

iam-ec2-role-without-instances.name: Iam Ec2 Role Without Instances
iam-ec2-role-without-instances.description: An IAM role intended for EC2 use is attached to no instances. The flagged IAM object isn't an immediate exposure but it muddies the access surface — unused groups, support-role gaps, dangling roles attached to no instance — and access reviews work better when the inventory matches reality.
iam-ec2-role-without-instances.risk: Stale IAM clutter masks real findings. Reviewers stop trusting the access list when it's clearly out of date, and an over-permissioned role tucked among twenty abandoned ones is much harder to spot. The fix is small and the audit-hygiene payoff is meaningful.

iam-group-with-inline-policies.name: Iam Group With Inline Policies
iam-group-with-inline-policies.description: An IAM group carries inline policies instead of attached managed policies. Inline IAM policies are harder to audit and reuse than managed policies; AWS guidance is to factor reusable rules into customer-managed policies, attach them by name, and reserve inline policies for genuinely one-off cases.
iam-group-with-inline-policies.risk: Inline policies tend to drift — each owner edits their own copy in isolation, review tooling has nothing stable to diff against, and a permission widened on one role is invisible until somebody runs an access audit. The result is a slow creep toward over-permissive access.

iam-group-with-no-users.name: Iam Group With No Users
iam-group-with-no-users.description: An IAM group has no users assigned. The flagged IAM object isn't an immediate exposure but it muddies the access surface — unused groups, support-role gaps, dangling roles attached to no instance — and access reviews work better when the inventory matches reality.
iam-group-with-no-users.risk: Stale IAM clutter masks real findings. Reviewers stop trusting the access list when it's clearly out of date, and an over-permissioned role tucked among twenty abandoned ones is much harder to spot. The fix is small and the audit-hygiene payoff is meaningful.

iam-inline-policy-allows-NotActions.name: Iam Inline Policy Allows Notactions
iam-inline-policy-allows-NotActions.description: An inline policy uses NotAction to define an allow list (over-broad by construction). The flagged policy grants more authority than the workload appears to need (full-action wildcards, NotAction-style allow lists, or unbounded resource selectors). The principle of least privilege requires the policy be narrowed to the specific actions and resources the workload actually uses.
iam-inline-policy-allows-NotActions.risk: Over-broad IAM permissions turn any single compromised credential into a foothold for lateral movement across the account. Attackers routinely enumerate attached policies and pivot from one over-permissioned role to another to reach data, secrets, or billing.

iam-inline-policy-allows-non-sts-action.name: Iam Inline Policy Allows Non Sts Action
iam-inline-policy-allows-non-sts-action.description: An inline policy on an STS-only context grants non-STS actions. The flagged policy grants more authority than the workload appears to need (full-action wildcards, NotAction-style allow lists, or unbounded resource selectors). The principle of least privilege requires the policy be narrowed to the specific actions and resources the workload actually uses.
iam-inline-policy-allows-non-sts-action.risk: Over-broad IAM permissions turn any single compromised credential into a foothold for lateral movement across the account. iam-inline-policy-for-role.name: Iam Inline Policy For Role
iam-inline-policy-for-role.description: An IAM role carries an inline policy rather than a managed attachment. Inline IAM policies are harder to audit and reuse than managed policies; AWS guidance is to factor reusable rules into customer-managed policies, attach them by name, and reserve inline policies for genuinely one-off cases.
iam-inline-policy-for-role.risk: Inline policies tend to drift — each owner edits their own copy in isolation, review tooling has nothing stable to diff against, and a permission widened on one role is invisible until somebody runs an access audit. The result is a slow creep toward over-permissive access.

iam-lightspin-user-action-denied-for-gro.name: Iam Lightspin User Action Denied For Group

The flagged policy grants more authority than the workload appears to need (full-action wildcards, NotAction-style allow lists, or unbounded resource selectors). The principle of least privilege requires the policy be narrowed to the specific actions and resources the workload actually uses.
iam-lightspin-user-action-denied-for-gro.risk: Over-broad IAM permissions turn any single compromised credential into a foothold for lateral movement across the account. Attackers routinely enumerate attached policies and pivot from one over-permissioned role to another to reach data, secrets, or billing.
iam-lightspin-user-action-denied-for-gro.remediation: Define all relevant users in the resource field of the affected policies to avoid ineffective IAM actions and deny all group actions. The alternative would be to use the condition "iam:ResourceTag" in the policy.

iam-password-policy-expiration-threshold.name: Iam Password Policy Expiration Threshold
iam-password-policy-expiration-threshold.description: The IAM account password policy does not enforce a password expiration period above the recommended threshold. AWS evaluates the policy against every console password set or reset by a human user; gaps in the policy translate directly into weaker credentials sitting in production.
iam-password-policy-expiration-threshold.risk: Weakened password policy makes credential-guessing attacks (password spray, dictionary lookup, reuse across services) materially cheaper. Compromised console credentials are the most common starting point for AWS account takeover incidents.
iam-password-policy-expiration-threshold.remediation: Enable password expiration and set the expiration period to 90 days or less

iam-root-account-no-hardware-mfa.name: Iam Root Account No Hardware Mfa
iam-root-account-no-hardware-mfa.description: The root account has no hardware MFA device enrolled. AWS does not require multi-factor authentication on a console- or API-facing principal by default — it has to be enforced via IAM policy, Identity Center, or an organization-wide SCP. The flagged principal is currently protected by a single factor.
iam-root-account-no-hardware-mfa.risk: Single-factor authentication on an AWS principal is one phishing email or one leaked-password-database hit away from a full takeover. AWS incident response data consistently shows missing MFA as the top initial-access vector for cloud account compromise.
iam-root-account-no-hardware-mfa.remediation: Enable MFA for the root account

iam-root-account-used-recently.name: Iam Root Account Used Recently
iam-root-account-used-recently.description: The root account was used recently. The AWS root account holds permanent, unrevocable authority over the billing, security, and resource state of the account; AWS guidance is to lock it down to a hardware MFA-protected break-glass identity and use IAM (or Identity Center) for routine work.
iam-root-account-used-recently.risk: Compromise of the root account is essentially unrecoverable — root credentials can disable CloudTrail, rotate keys, and create new admins faster than any automated control can react. Every active root credential, every recent root sign-in, and every missing root MFA increases the blast radius of one phish.
iam-root-account-used-recently.remediation: Follow the remediation instructions of the Ensure IAM policies are attached only to groups or roles recommendation

iam-unused-credentials-not-disabled.name: Iam Unused Credentials Not Disabled
iam-unused-credentials-not-disabled.description: IAM credentials that have been unused for a long window remain active. Long-lived access keys and passwords accumulate exposure: every backup tape, every old git commit, every ex-employee laptop is another place the credential might be reachable. AWS guidance is to rotate every 90 days and to delete keys that have been unused for a comparable window.
iam-unused-credentials-not-disabled.risk: Static long-lived credentials are the bulk of the cloud-account-compromise fixture — leaked S3 buckets, GitHub commits, container images. The longer a key lives, the more accidental copies of it exist. Multiple keys per user multiplies the attack surface for no operational gain.
iam-unused-credentials-not-disabled.remediation: Ensure that all credentials (including passwords and access keys) have been used and changed in the last _ARG_0_ days

iam-user-no-key-rotation.remediation: Rotate access keys that have not been changed recently

kms-cmk-rotation-disabled.name: Kms Cmk Rotation Disabled
kms-cmk-rotation-disabled.description: A customer-managed KMS CMK has automatic rotation disabled. AWS provides encryption-at-rest as a first-class default for every storage service involved here (S3, EBS, RDS, Redshift, DynamoDB, SQS, …) via either SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, or a customer-managed CMK. The flagged resource is storing data unencrypted at the storage layer.
kms-cmk-rotation-disabled.risk: Unencrypted storage means a snapshot leak, a disk-level forensic recovery, or a misrouted backup yields data in plaintext. KMS-based encryption also lets the key policy revoke access en-masse during incident response — without it, key rotation is meaningless.
kms-cmk-rotation-disabled.remediation: For every KMS Customer Master Keys (CMKs), ensure that Rotate this key every year is enabled

logs-no-alarm-aws-configuration-changes.name: Logs No Alarm Aws Configuration Changes
logs-no-alarm-aws-configuration-changes.description: No CloudWatch alarm fires on AWS Config changes. CIS-recommended CloudWatch alarms cover the security-relevant API surface — IAM changes, sign-in failures, root usage, NACL/SG/route-table edits, CMK deletion. Without alarms wired to a real notification channel the audit log is data nobody reads.
logs-no-alarm-aws-configuration-changes.risk: Detection coverage is the gap between an attacker's first move and the responder's first action. Missing alarms on the API calls that signal compromise (CreateUser, DeleteTrail, root-account sign-in, unauthorized API calls) extend the window where an intrusion goes unanswered.
logs-no-alarm-aws-configuration-changes.remediation: Enable a CloudWatch alarm to detect AWS configuration changes.

logs-no-alarm-route-table-changes.description: No CloudWatch alarm fires on route-table changes. CIS-recommended CloudWatch alarms cover the security-relevant API surface — IAM changes, sign-in failures, root usage, NACL/SG/route-table edits, CMK deletion. Without alarms wired to a real notification channel the audit log is data nobody reads.
logs-no-alarm-route-table-changes.risk: Detection coverage is the gap between an attacker's first move and the responder's first action. Missing alarms on the API calls that signal compromise (CreateUser, DeleteTrail, root-account sign-in, unauthorized API calls) extend the window where an intrusion goes unanswered.
logs-no-alarm-route-table-changes.remediation: Enable a CloudWatch alarm to detect route table changes.

logs-no-alarm-s3-policy-changes.name: Logs No Alarm S3 Policy Changes
logs-no-alarm-s3-policy-changes.description: No CloudWatch alarm fires on S3 bucket policy changes. CIS-recommended CloudWatch alarms cover the security-relevant API surface — IAM changes, sign-in failures, root usage, NACL/SG/route-table edits, CMK deletion. Without alarms wired to a real notification channel the audit log is data nobody reads.
logs-no-alarm-s3-policy-changes.risk: Detection coverage is the gap between an attacker's first move and the responder's first action. logs-no-alarm-s3-policy-changes.remediation: Enable a CloudWatch alarm to detect policy changes in S3 buckets.

logs-no-alarm-security-group-changes.name: Logs No Alarm Security Group Changes
logs-no-alarm-security-group-changes.description: No CloudWatch alarm fires on security-group changes. The security group rule is wider than the workload behind it needs — a `0.0.0.0/0` ingress on an administrative port, a default SG carrying real rules, or a long-unused SG kept around as dead config. AWS guidance is to scope every rule to specific CIDRs, principals, or SG references.
logs-no-alarm-security-group-changes.risk: Wide-open security group rules give attackers direct network reach to backend services that were never hardened for the public internet — SSH brute force, RDP attacks, exposed database ports, unauthenticated metadata endpoints. The exposure persists 24/7.
logs-no-alarm-security-group-changes.remediation: Enable a CloudWatch alarm to monitor security group changes.

logs-no-alarm-signin-without-mfa.name: Logs No Alarm Signin Without Mfa
logs-no-alarm-signin-without-mfa.description: No CloudWatch alarm fires on console sign-in without MFA. AWS does not require multi-factor authentication on a console- or API-facing principal by default — it has to be enforced via IAM policy, Identity Center, or an organization-wide SCP. The flagged principal is currently protected by a single factor.
logs-no-alarm-signin-without-mfa.risk: Single-factor authentication on an AWS principal is one phishing email or one leaked-password-database hit away from a full takeover. AWS incident response data consistently shows missing MFA as the top initial-access vector for cloud account compromise.
logs-no-alarm-signin-without-mfa.remediation: Enable a CloudWatch alarm to monitor console log ins without MFA.

logs-no-alarm-unauthorized-api-calls.remediation: Enable a CloudWatch alarm to monitor unauthorized API calls.

rds-instance-backup-disabled.name: Rds Instance Backup Disabled
rds-instance-backup-disabled.description: The RDS instance has automated backups disabled. AWS-managed data stores ship automatic backup and recovery primitives (snapshots, PITR, cross-AZ replicas, versioning, deletion protection) but they have to be enabled per-resource. Default-off behaviors leave the resource one operator-error or one ransomware event away from data loss.
rds-instance-backup-disabled.risk: A resource without backups, retention, or deletion protection is a single incident from permanent loss — and the incident isn't usually malicious. Accidental DROP TABLE, accidental terraform destroy, accidental S3 object overwrite all produce the same blast radius.

rds-instance-ca-certificate-deprecated.name: Rds Instance Ca Certificate Deprecated
rds-instance-ca-certificate-deprecated.description: The RDS instance uses a deprecated certificate authority. The TLS configuration on the flagged endpoint accepts protocol versions or cipher suites AWS has deprecated, or relies on a certificate the browser/AWS trust store no longer accepts. AWS publishes managed security policies that track current guidance — using one is the supported path.
rds-instance-ca-certificate-deprecated.risk: Old TLS versions (1.0/1.1) and weak ciphers are vulnerable to downgrade and padding-oracle attacks that retrieve plaintext from intercepted traffic. Deprecated certificates fail validation as clients update, eventually causing a production outage on top of the security gap.

rds-instance-no-minor-upgrade.name: Rds Instance No Minor Upgrade
rds-instance-no-minor-upgrade.description: The RDS instance has auto minor-version upgrades disabled. The flagged AWS-managed component is on a deprecated version — an engine release with known CVEs, a certificate authority the trust store is phasing out, or an auto-upgrade path the workload has opted out of. The supported path is to apply the upgrade in a maintenance window.
rds-instance-no-minor-upgrade.risk: Software running on outdated AWS-managed engines is exposed to whatever CVEs the upgrade fixes — typically authentication bypasses, privilege escalation in stored procedures, or TLS-layer vulnerabilities. The longer the lag, the longer the window of exposure and the more disruptive the eventual catch-up upgrade.

rds-instance-publicly-accessible.name: Rds Instance Publicly Accessible
rds-instance-publicly-accessible.description: The RDS instance is publicly accessible. The flagged resource is reachable from outside the AWS account or exposes data to anonymous principals (`Principal: "*"`, a public ACL, a publicly routable IP, or a public snapshot). AWS guidance is to confine the resource to a private subnet, a specific account, or a named principal list.
rds-instance-publicly-accessible.risk: Publicly exposed AWS resources are continuously scanned by attackers — Shodan, censys, and dedicated bucket-enumeration tools find them within minutes of exposure. The most common AWS data breaches start as misconfigured public buckets or snapshots.

rds-instance-short-backup-retention-peri.name: Rds Instance Short Backup Retention Period
rds-instance-short-backup-retention-peri.description: The RDS automated-backup retention window is shorter than recommended. AWS-managed data stores ship automatic backup and recovery primitives (snapshots, PITR, cross-AZ replicas, versioning, deletion protection) but they have to be enabled per-resource. Default-off behaviors leave the resource one operator-error or one ransomware event away from data loss.
rds-instance-short-backup-retention-peri.risk: A resource without backups, retention, or deletion protection is a single incident from permanent loss — and the incident isn't usually malicious. Accidental DROP TABLE, accidental terraform destroy, accidental S3 object overwrite all produce the same blast radius.

rds-instance-single-az.name: Rds Instance Single Az
rds-instance-single-az.description: The RDS instance is single-AZ (no multi-AZ failover). AWS-managed data stores ship automatic backup and recovery primitives (snapshots, PITR, cross-AZ replicas, versioning, deletion protection) but they have to be enabled per-resource. Default-off behaviors leave the resource one operator-error or one ransomware event away from data loss.
rds-instance-single-az.risk: A resource without backups, retention, or deletion protection is a single incident from permanent loss — and the incident isn't usually malicious. Accidental DROP TABLE, accidental terraform destroy, accidental S3 object overwrite all produce the same blast radius.

rds-instance-storage-not-encrypted.name: Rds Instance Storage Not Encrypted
rds-instance-storage-not-encrypted.description: The RDS instance storage is unencrypted at rest. AWS provides encryption-at-rest as a first-class default for every storage service involved here (S3, EBS, RDS, Redshift, DynamoDB, SQS, …) via either SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, or a customer-managed CMK. The flagged resource is storing data unencrypted at the storage layer.
rds-instance-storage-not-encrypted.risk: Unencrypted storage means a snapshot leak, a disk-level forensic recovery, or a misrouted backup yields data in plaintext. KMS-based encryption also lets the key policy revoke access en-masse during incident response — without it, key rotation is meaningless.

rds-postgres-instance-with-invalid-certi.name: Rds Postgres Instance With Invalid Certificate
rds-postgres-instance-with-invalid-certi.description: An RDS PostgreSQL client is configured with an invalid server certificate. The TLS configuration on the flagged endpoint accepts protocol versions or cipher suites AWS has deprecated, or relies on a certificate the browser/AWS trust store no longer accepts. AWS publishes managed security policies that track current guidance — using one is the supported path.
rds-postgres-instance-with-invalid-certi.risk: Old TLS versions (1.0/1.1) and weak ciphers are vulnerable to downgrade and padding-oracle attacks that retrieve plaintext from intercepted traffic. Deprecated certificates fail validation as clients update, eventually causing a production outage on top of the security gap.

redshift-parameter-group-logging-disable.description: A Redshift parameter group has audit logging disabled. AWS audit and access-log streams (CloudTrail, S3 access logs, ELB access logs, VPC flow logs, Config) are off or missing on the flagged surface. AWS guidance is to centralize these into a dedicated security-account S3 bucket with KMS encryption and integrity validation.

route53-domain-no-autorenew.name: Route53 Domain No Autorenew
route53-domain-no-autorenew.description: A Route 53 registered domain has auto-renew disabled. Route 53 domain registration carries operational controls that protect against accidental loss of the registration — auto-renew, transfer lock, and registrar-side transfer authorization. Lapses cost the entire DNS hierarchy rooted at the domain.
route53-domain-no-autorenew.risk: Loss of a registered domain is unique among AWS findings in that it can take down email, web, SSO, and customer-facing services simultaneously, often with limited recovery options. Domain-takeover incidents are rare but functionally catastrophic when they occur.

s3-bucket-allowing-cleartext.name: S3 Bucket Allowing Cleartext
s3-bucket-allowing-cleartext.description: An S3 bucket policy allows cleartext HTTP requests. The flagged endpoint accepts unencrypted HTTP / cleartext traffic — either as the only protocol, or alongside HTTPS without a redirect. AWS guidance is to terminate TLS at the edge and force every connection through it.
s3-bucket-allowing-cleartext.risk: Cleartext traffic means anyone on the network path — a misconfigured wifi captive portal, a hostile cafe, a downstream router — can read or modify the payload. Even endpoints that only carry public data leak credentials in cookies, auth headers, and query strings.

s3-bucket-no-default-encryption.description: An S3 bucket has no default encryption configured. AWS provides encryption-at-rest as a first-class default for every storage service involved here (S3, EBS, RDS, Redshift, DynamoDB, SQS, …) via either SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, or a customer-managed CMK. s3-bucket-no-logging.name: S3 Bucket No Logging

s3-bucket-no-logging.remediation: Ensure that S3 buckets have Logging enabled

s3-bucket-world-policy-star.name: S3 Bucket World Policy Star
s3-bucket-world-policy-star.description: An S3 bucket policy uses Principal: "*" (publicly accessible). The flagged resource is reachable from outside the AWS account or exposes data to anonymous principals (`Principal: "*"`, a public ACL, a publicly routable IP, or a public snapshot). AWS guidance is to confine the resource to a private subnet, a specific account, or a named principal list.
s3-bucket-world-policy-star.risk: Publicly exposed AWS resources are continuously scanned by attackers — Shodan, censys, and dedicated bucket-enumeration tools find them within minutes of exposure. The most common AWS data breaches start as misconfigured public buckets or snapshots.
s3-bucket-world-policy-star.remediation: Remove any Statement having an Effect set to Allow and a Principal set to "*" or {"AWS":"*"} in the affected bucket policy

ses-identity-dkim-not-enabled.name: Ses Identity Dkim Not Enabled
ses-identity-dkim-not-enabled.description: An SES sending identity has DKIM signing disabled. SES domain identity DKIM signing authenticates outbound mail to downstream receivers; without it, mail is easier to spoof and gets penalized on reputation. AWS guidance is to enable DKIM on every verified sending identity.
ses-identity-dkim-not-enabled.risk: Missing DKIM lets attackers send convincingly-spoofed mail from the domain — phishing campaigns leveraging executive identities are an established initial-access pattern. Receivers also penalize unauthenticated mail, so legitimate transactional email starts landing in spam.

ses-identity-dkim-not-verified.name: Ses Identity Dkim Not Verified
ses-identity-dkim-not-verified.description: An SES sending identity's DKIM configuration is unverified. AWS guidance is to enable DKIM on every verified sending identity.

Receivers also penalize unauthenticated mail, so legitimate transactional email starts landing in spam.

vpc-routing-tables-with-peering.name: Vpc Routing Tables With Peering
vpc-routing-tables-with-peering.description: A VPC route table mixes peering routes with non-peering destinations. The VPC / networking surface flagged here is wider or noisier than the workload requires: public IPs on resources that don't need them, VPC peering without scoped routes, subnets without flow logs to inspect traffic. AWS guidance is to default to private subnets and add visibility (flow logs, GWLB) before adding reachability.
vpc-routing-tables-with-peering.risk: Network exposure compounds — a public IP plus a wide security group plus an unpatched service is the standard chain leading to AWS instance compromise. Missing flow logs then deprive responders of the data needed to bound the blast radius after the fact.
vpc-routing-tables-with-peering.remediation: Ensure route tables contain the least number of subnets or hosts as is required to accomplish the purpose for peering

vpc-subnet-without-flow-log.name: Vpc Subnet Without Flow Log
vpc-subnet-without-flow-log.description: A VPC subnet has no VPC Flow Logs configured. AWS audit and access-log streams (CloudTrail, S3 access logs, ELB access logs, VPC flow logs, Config) are off or missing on the flagged surface. AWS guidance is to centralize these into a dedicated security-account S3 bucket with KMS encryption and integrity validation.
vpc-subnet-without-flow-log.risk: Without complete logs, every post-incident investigation hits a wall — there is no record of who did what, when, or from where. Compliance frameworks treat missing audit trail as a high-severity finding because it forecloses detection AND response.
vpc-subnet-without-flow-log.remediation: Create a flow log for each subnet.
